title: Rabbit emergency care and basic first aid
subtitle: Recognising GI stasis, hypothermia, and bite wounds, and knowing when to call a vet.
excerpt: Rabbits can decline very fast. Knowing the warning signs of GI stasis, hypothermia, and predator injuries helps you act quickly and get a rabbit to a vet before the window closes.
sourceNotes: House Rabbit Society, GI stasis information sheets. RWAF emergency care guidance. Merck Veterinary Manual, rabbit gastrointestinal and emergency sections.

glossaryTerms.definition: Gastrointestinal stasis. A condition in which the gut slows or stops moving. Gas builds up rapidly. Without treatment, GI stasis is fatal within 24 to 48 hours. It is the most common rabbit emergency.

The movement of the intestinal muscles that push food through the digestive tract. In rabbits, gut motility must remain constant. Any disruption, from pain, stress, dehydration, or low fibre, can trigger stasis.
A dangerously low body temperature. A rabbit's normal temperature is 38.5 to 40 C. Below 37 C the rabbit is at serious risk. Signs include cold ears and feet, limpness, and unresponsiveness.

content.text: Rabbits are prey animals. They hide illness until they can no longer do so. By the time a rabbit looks unwell, it is already in trouble. Knowing what to look for during daily checks can save a life.
GI stasis: recognition and response
GI stasis
is the most common rabbit emergency. The gut slows or stops. Gas accumulates and causes severe pain. Watch for these signs:
Stopped eating. This is the first and clearest sign. A rabbit that is not eating hay is not well.
No droppings, or very few small ones. Check the litter tray at each feed.
Hunched posture. The rabbit sits pressed down with eyes half-closed.
Teeth grinding. A low grinding sound (bruxism) signals pain.
A hard or bloated abdomen when you gently palpate.
If you see these signs, call a vet immediately. Do not wait to see if the rabbit improves overnight.
Gut motility
must be restored quickly. While waiting for the vet, keep the rabbit warm, encourage gentle movement, and offer water by syringe if the rabbit will not drink. Do not force-feed food.
Hypothermia
Hypothermia
can occur in wet, cold, or draughty conditions and also after a shock such as a predator attack. Signs are cold ears and paws, limpness, shallow breathing, and unresponsiveness.

Bring the rabbit indoors at once. Wrap it in a towel. Place a warm (not hot) water bottle wrapped in a cloth next to the body, not under it. Direct heat on bare skin will burn. Warm the rabbit slowly over 20 to 30 minutes. Once the rabbit is alert and warm, call the vet for a check. Internal injury from cold can take several hours to become apparent.
Bite wounds

Bite wounds from cats, dogs, or foxes are often deceptively small on the surface. The animal's teeth penetrate deep into tissue and introduce bacteria. A tiny puncture wound on the back or flank is a vet emergency.

Do not try to clean deep punctures yourself. Cover the wound loosely with a clean cloth and get to a vet within the hour. Antibiotics and treatment for internal bruising or organ damage are usually needed. Even a rabbit that appears to walk normally after a bite can have internal injuries.
When to go to the vet urgently
Go to a vet without delay in any of these situations:
Not eating for 4 hours or more.
No droppings for 4 hours.
Any bite wound, however small.
Signs of hypothermia.
Head tilt, rolling, or loss of balance.
Paralysed or dragging hind legs.
Laboured or open-mouthed breathing.
Find a rabbit-savvy vet before you need one. Not every small-animal vet has rabbit experience. Ask directly when you register your animals.
